checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed both the source PDF and the converted JSON output. All headings, agenda items, sub-items, and meeting details in the JSON are directly derived from the source document. There are no fabricated paragraphs or sentences. AI-generated metadata like the title and alt text for images are allowed per the guidelines.

The meeting details include a phone number (984-204-1487) and a Conference ID (766 174 851#). I compared these values between the source PDF and the JSON output. The phone number and conference ID match exactly.

The source document consists of two pages. Section 1 of the JSON contains the information from page 1 (Agenda items 1-6), and Section 2 contains the information from page 2 (Agenda items 7-13, Adjourn, and the footer note). Both pages and all agenda sections are represented.

The agenda items on Page 1 are correctly structured in a table within the JSON, including sub-items for the Consent Agenda (Tab 5) and Executive Director Report (Tab 6). Page 2 continues the list of items 7-13, which are represented as paragraphs; this change in layout is an acceptable reformatting that preserves all content.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I verified the financial data in Tab 5 (NG 911 Fund, CMRS Fund, PSAP Fund, Grant Fund) including month-end balances and disbursements. All dollar values match the source exactly. Sub-items for Tabs 5 and 6 are fully present. No significant text omissions were found.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text content matches the source document with high fidelity. Formatting indicators like asterisks used for bold/italic text for the '(Roll Call Vote Required)' labels are acceptable reformatting. Names of presenters and committee titles are accurate.

checks.a_analysis: The output accurately represents the content of the source document without duplication. Each agenda item and section appears only once, matching the source PDF structure.
The heading hierarchy is logical. 'AGENDA' is correctly assigned as H1, and the meeting details follow as H2. No heading levels are skipped.

The document uses a 3-column layout (Tab, Topic, Presenter) for all agenda items across both pages. On Page 1, the pipeline correctly identifies and renders this as a table. However, on Page 2 (items 7-13), the structural organization is lost. Instead of continuing the table structure or starting a new one, the 3-column content is merged into single paragraph strings (e.g., merging item number, committee name, and presenters into one line). This is a major structural failure as the column data is no longer distinct.
checks.b_evidence_source: The content on Page 2 follows the same 3-column alignment as Page 1: '7. Education Committee Report.............................................Chuck Greene/Angie Turbeville'
checks.b_evidence_output: The output renders this as a single paragraph: "7. Education Committee Report.............................................Chuck Greene/Angie Turbeville"

checks.a_analysis: The reading order follows the logical flow of the original agenda from top to bottom, including the transition between pages.

The list items on Page 2 (7-13) are rendered as flat paragraph elements rather than being contained within a semantic list structure. While the text numbering is preserved, the structural meaning of a list is lost.
checks.b_evidence_source: Numbered list items 7 through 13 on page 2.
checks.b_evidence_output: Individual 'type': 'paragraph' elements for each list item (elements 5-12 in section 2).

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains no explicit hyperlinked URLs, and the output does not incorrectly inject or fabricate any links. Contact information like the phone number is preserved as text.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the document is significantly altered. In the source PDF, the agenda is a continuous list (items 1-13) with a consistent three-column visual alignment. In the HTML, items 1-6 are rendered inside a bordered table, while items 7-13 are rendered as plain text paragraphs, breaking visual consistency. Additionally, the centered logo on page 2 of the source PDF is positioned between items 11 and 12; in the HTML, this logo has been moved to appear before item 7, disrupting the logical flow of the agenda items.
checks.b_evidence_source: The agenda items 1-13 share a consistent column-aligned layout. Item 11 is followed by the logo, which is then followed by item 12.
checks.b_evidence_output: Items 1-6 are in a table with a grey header; Items 7-13 are rendered as plain text below a large logo image which appears before item 7.
checks.b_evidence_location: Throughout the document, specifically the transition between item 6 and 7, and the placement of the logo image.

checks.a_analysis: The table used for items 1-6 is visually intact and readable. Columns are aligned, and the content is distributed logically within cells. While the failure to use a table for the remainder of the list is a layout consistency issue (V1), the table that is present is not corrupted or broken.

The 'NC DIT 911 Board' branding logo located at the top-left of the first page in the source PDF is missing from the header area of the HTML output. While a version of this logo appears later in the document, its omission from the primary document header is a defect in representing meaningful source images.
checks.b_evidence_source: Top-left corner of Page 1: NC DIT 911 Board logo.
checks.b_evidence_output: The top of the HTML document (header area) contains only text and is missing the logo image.

checks.a_analysis: There is no visual evidence of duplicated body content. Each paragraph, list item, and table row appears only once in the sequence corresponding to the source.
